facts.text: Jackson Heights got its name from John Jackson, president of the Hunters Point and Flushing Turnpike Company, who ran the Jackson Avenue trolley.
Perched east of Woodside and north of Corona, Jackson Heights is one of New York City’s most influential planned communities and a lasting multicultural center.
In 1993, the city designated nearly 600 buildings in Jackson Heights as a historic district, preserving its garden‑city architecture.
During the 1920s, LGBT actors from the 42nd Street theater scene moved to Jackson Heights, drawn by affordable housing and easy access to the 7 train.
Jackson Heights was founded in 1919 and officially dedicated in 1923 as part of the Queensboro Corporation’s planned development.
